scenario: Crestline Insurance has a Camunda 8.8 process where the root scope already has a variable named riskLevel for each of the 3,000 claims processed daily. A worker completes a job inside a nested subprocess and returns riskLevel together with a brand-new variable claimNotes. The team needs to predict where each variable will end up after the merge.
question: Where do riskLevel and claimNotes reside after the job completes in the nested subprocess?

options.text: The engine updates the existing riskLevel in the root scope where propagation stops, and creates claimNotes as a new variable in the root scope because no parent scope already has it.
The engine creates both riskLevel and claimNotes as local variables in the nested subprocess scope, leaving the existing root-scope riskLevel untouched until the subprocess scope ends.
The engine updates the existing riskLevel only in the nested subprocess scope and creates claimNotes there as well, so neither of the two changes is visible once the subprocess completes.
The engine rejects the job completion because a worker is not allowed to write to a variable that already exists in a higher scope of the same process instance during a single merge.

a.text: Correct. Propagation walks each variable up until it finds a scope already containing the name; riskLevel stops at the root scope, and claimNotes finds no match so it is created in the root scope.

b.text: Incorrect. Variables returned by job completion are propagated to higher scopes by default; only an explicit local-variable mechanism would keep them confined to the nested subprocess scope.

c.text: Incorrect. riskLevel's update stops in the root scope where the name first exists, and claimNotes is created in the root scope rather than in the nested subprocess scope by default.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no rule that rejects job completion when a returned variable matches a higher-scope variable; the engine simply handles the propagation according to the documented merge rules.

explanation: Variable propagation on job completion walks each returned variable from the activity scope toward the root scope. It stops at the first scope that already contains a variable with the same name and updates it; if no scope contains it, the engine creates the variable in the root scope.
